content.full_text:
COUNTY OF LOS ANGELES
OFFICE OF THE COUNTY COUNSEL
648 HALL OF ADMINISTRATION
500 WEST TEMPLE STREET
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90012
TELEPHONE

DE WITT W. CLINTON, COUNTY COUNSEL                                    (213) 974-1827
                                                                      [illegible]
                             April 15, 1992
                                                                      (213) 680-2165

Ben Davidian, Chairman
and Members of the Commission
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804-0807

     Re:  Request for Opinion

Dear Chairman Davidian and Commissioners:

     We are writing to seek written advice on behalf of the
Los Angeles County Employee Retirement Association ("LACERA")
regarding the application of the Political Reform Act ("Act")
to officers and employees of LACERA who also serve as
officers of title holding corporations owned and controlled
by LACERA.

     LACERA is a county pension association established under
the County Employees Retirement Law of 1937 (California
Government Code Section 31450 et seq.). The purpose of
LACERA and its counterparts is to provide retirement and
disability benefits to county and district employees.

     In carrying out its statutory mandate, LACERA has made
certain investments in real property. On the advice of its
outside consultants and legal counsel, LACERA has determined
it to be prudent to hold title to these real property
investments in title holding corporations. These title
holding corporations are owned and controlled by LACERA and,
under applicable federal and California state law, are not
subject to income taxation or property tax assessment. The
purpose of these title holding subsidiaries is simply to hold
and operate real property investments for the benefit of
LACERA and its beneficiaries. Under applicable Federal tax
law, such title holding corporations are prohibited from
engaging in any trade or business. I.R.C. Section 501(c)(2)
and (c)(25). (Attached as Attachments 1, 2 and 3 hereto are
applicable federal, state and county determinations
reflecting the tax exempt status of LACERA's title holding
corporations.)

Ben Davidian and Members of the Commission
Page 2


     A question has arisen under the Act regarding the fact
that LACERA uses its senior officers to serve as the officers
and directors of its title holding corporations.  This
staffing arrangement is in LACERA's best interest as it
enables LACERA to maintain these title holding corporations
under its strict supervision and control and to have
directors and officers who enjoy the confidence of and who
are both responsible to and responsive to LACERA's Board of
Trustees.  However, it has also given rise to an issue as to
whether these senior officers are limited in the extent to
which they may participate as part of their LACERA duties in
decisions regarding the management of the title holding
corporations as a consequence of their status as officers and
directors of such corporations.

     In order to clarify this issue, we are requesting the
Commission's advice on the following issue.

     Does the appointment by LACERA of its officers or
employees to serve, as a part of their positions or
employment with LACERA and for no additional
compensation, as directors and officers of wholly-owned
title holding corporations, which have no business or
function other than the holding and management of
LACERA's real property assets, require such persons to
disclose these positions on their statements of economic
interests or to refrain from or disqualify themselves
from participating in LACERA's decisions with respect to
such title holding corporations?

     It is our opinion that no conflict of interest exists
since the individuals are, in fact, serving as officers of
the title holding corporations as a corollary to their
responsibilities with LACERA.  And, there is no possibility
that they will derive any personal gain, financial or
otherwise, from any decisions they, functioning as officers
of LACERA, may make regarding the title holding corporation.
Finally, to limit the ability of these senior officers to
perform their functions on behalf of LACERA would be
counterproductive and not in the best interests of LACERA or
its members.  Therefore, the Act should not be interpreted so
as to require such a result.

     We believe that the situation is analogous to In the
Matter Of:  Opinion Requested By Richard E. Sherwood,
President, Board of Trustees, Los Angeles County Museum of

Ben Davidian and Members of the Commission
Page 3


Arts, No. 76-038, December 15, 1976.  Therein, the Commission
noted that although Museum Associates, Inc., constituted a
"source of income" under Government Code Section 87103, the
relationship between Museum Associates, Inc., and the Los
Angeles County Department of Museum of Art was such that the
purposes of Government Code Section 87100 would not be served
by imposing any general prohibition on the participation of
such public officials in decision making with respect to the
County Museum of Fine Arts.

     Applying the Commission's analysis in that opinion to
the instant situation, we believe that LACERA's facts even
more strongly compel the conclusion that the Act does not
apply to such situations. We note that:

     -    The public officials serve as directors and
          officers of LACERA's title holding corporations at
          the request of LACERA, as a part of their positions
          or employment with LACERA, and receive no separate
          compensation for these services.

     -    The title holding corporations are wholly owned by
          LACERA and are established for administrative
          convenience and to afford an additional level of
          protection to the general assets of LACERA from
          liabilities specific to the properties held by its
          title holding corporations.

     -    All earnings and profits of LACERA's title holding
          corporations inure to the benefit of LACERA.

     -    Under applicable California law, the officers and
          directors of a title holding corporation owe a duty
          of due care and loyalty to their stockholders
          which, in this case, would be LACERA.¹


___________________________

     ¹Farmer's & Merchants Bank of Los Angeles v. Downey, 53
Cal. 466, 31 Am.R. 62 (1879) (Corporation directors act in
fiduciary capacity and are stockholders' trustees);
Professional Hockey Corp. vs. World Hockey Association, 143
Cal.App. 3d 410, 191 Cal.Rptr. 773 (Ct. App. 1983) (In
performance of their official duties, directors are under
obligations of trust and confidence to the corporation and
its stockholders, and they must act in good faith and with
due care, diligence and loyalty for the interests of the
corporation or stockholders).

Ben Davidian and Members of the Commission
Page 4


-    The directors of each of LACERA's title holding
     corporations serve at the pleasure of LACERA and
     may be removed at any time with or without cause.
     The officers of each of LACERA's title holding
     corporations serve at the pleasure of the directors
     and may likewise be removed at any time with or
     without cause.

-    The United States government, the State of
     California and the County of Los Angeles have each
     recognized that LACERA's title holding corporations
     are, in essence, part and parcel of LACERA, and
     that they have no separate and independent business
     function other than holding title to and operating
     LACERA's real property investments.

     In light of the above stated facts, it is clear that
there is a complete commonality of interest between LACERA's
title holding corporations, their officers and directors and
LACERA. As in the case of Museum Associates, there is no
reason to fear a disqualifying conflict of interest. The
public officials involved receive all of their compensation
from LACERA and, as a matter of state law, owe a duty of care
and loyalty to LACERA, even when they are acting in
their capacity as officers and directors of LACERA's title
holding corporations.

     In summary, LACERA's title holding companies are not
"operated for profit" any more than LACERA itself is operated
for profit. In light of the statutory purpose of the Act as
set forth in Government Code sections 81001 and 81002, there
does not appear that any conflict of interest potential
for conflict of interest would result from LACERA's use of a
title holding corporation structure. These public officials
gain no benefit from serving as officers or directors of
LACERA's title holding corporations which is divisible from
their status as LACERA employees.

     Out an abundance of caution, pending your advice on this
issue, the LACERA officers who are serving as officers for a
title holding corporation have chosen to disclose their
positions with the corporation on their recently filed
statements of economic interests. While, for the reasons
stated above, we do not believe such disclosure was
necessary, we agree with LACERA that it was wise, as a

Ben Davidian and Members of the Commission
Page 5


precaution, to report these positions until your advice is
received.

     We appreciate your time in responding to our concerns.
Should you need any additional information, please feel free
to contact me at (213) 974-1866.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        DE WITT W. CLINTON
                                        County Counsel


                                        By Roberta Fesler /LAK
                                        ROBERTA M. FESLER
                                        Assistant County Counsel
                                        Public Services Division

LAK:fc
3.fppc

Enclosures

cc:  Steve Johnson
     Los Angeles County Employees
     Retirement Association

sections.question: Does the appointment by LACERA of its officers or employees to serve, as a part of their positions or employment with LACERA and for no additional compensation, as directors and officers of wholly-owned title holding corporations, which have no business or function other than the holding and management of LACERA's real property assets, require such persons to disclose these positions on their statements of economic interests or to refrain from or disqualify themselves from participating in LACERA's decisions with respect to such title holding corporations?
sections.question_synthetic: Whether LACERA officers and employees who serve as directors and officers of wholly-owned title holding corporations must disclose these positions on statements of economic interests or disqualify themselves from participating in LACERA decisions regarding such corporations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from Los Angeles County Counsel to the FPPC seeking advice. The letter contains the applicant's opinion (not FPPC's response) that no conflict exists, but this is not an FPPC conclusion. The document includes substantial legal analysis by the County Counsel but no FPPC response or formal conclusion. Confidence is high as the document clearly states the question being posed and the requestor's reasoning.

embedding.summary: The Los Angeles County Counsel requests FPPC advice regarding whether officers and employees of the Los Angeles County Employee Retirement Association (LACERA) who serve as directors and officers of LACERA's wholly-owned title holding corporations must disclose these positions or abstain from decisions related to the corporations. The County Counsel argues no conflict of interest exists because the individuals receive no separate compensation, the corporations are wholly owned by LACERA with no independent business function, and all earnings inure to LACERA's benefit.
